questionText: A, B and C started a business by investing $\rupee 120000$, $\rupee 135000$ and $\rupee 150000$ respectively. Find the share of each, out of an annual profit of $\rupee 56700$.
correctAnswer: A's share = ₹16800, B's share = ₹18900, C's share = ₹21000

steps.explanation: Find ratio of investments of A, B and C.
Total parts = 8 + 9 + 10 = 27. Distribute profit in this ratio.
Calculate B's share.
Calculate C's share.

solution.shortcut: In simple partnership, share of profit is directly proportional to capital invested.
solution.commonMistake: Not simplifying the ratio before dividing; or forgetting to verify shares sum to total profit.

questionText: Alfred started a business investing $\rupee 45000$. After 3 months, Peter joined him with a capital of $\rupee 60000$. After another 6 months, Ronald joined them with a capital of $\rupee 90000$. At the end of the year, they made a profit of $\rupee 16500$. Find the share of each.
correctAnswer: Alfred's share = ₹6600, Peter's share = ₹6600, Ronald's share = ₹3300

steps.explanation: Determine the time each partner's capital was invested. Alfred invested for 12 months, Peter for 9 months (joined after 3 months), Ronald for 3 months (joined after 9 months).
steps.calculation: Alfred: 12 months, Peter: 12−3=9 months, Ronald: 12−9=3 months

steps.explanation: Calculate effective capital (capital × time) for each partner.
Distribute profit of ₹16500 in ratio 2:2:1. Total parts = 5.

solution.shortcut: In compound partnership, use capital × time as the effective contribution for each partner.
solution.commonMistake: Using total months from start rather than actual months each partner invested.

formulas.title: Compound Partnership Profit Ratio
formulas.expression: \text{A's profit} : \text{B's profit} = x \cdot p : y \cdot q

questionText: A, B and C start a business each investing $\rupee 20000$. After 5 months A withdrew $\rupee 5000$, B withdrew $\rupee 4000$ and C invests $\rupee 6000$ more. At the end of the year, a total profit of $\rupee 69900$ was recorded. Find the share of each.
correctAnswer: A's share = ₹20500, B's share = ₹21200, C's share = ₹28200

steps.explanation: For each partner, split investment into two periods: first 5 months and remaining 7 months.
steps.calculation: A: 20000 for 5 months, then 15000 for 7 months. B: 20000 for 5 months, then 16000 for 7 months. C: 20000 for 5 months, then 26000 for 7 months.

steps.explanation: Calculate effective capital for each partner.
Total ratio parts = 205+212+282 = 699. Distribute profit ₹69900.

solution.shortcut: When capital changes mid-way, split the year into segments and compute capital × time for each segment, then sum for each partner.
solution.commonMistake: Adding or subtracting the changed amount from the wrong period, or not accounting for partial-year segments.

questionText: A, B and C enter into partnership. A invests 3 times as much as B invests and B invests two-thirds of what C invests. At the end of the year, the profit earned is $\rupee 6600$. What is the share of B?

steps.explanation: Let C's capital = ₹x. Then B's capital = (2/3)x and A's capital = 3 × (2/3)x = 2x.
Simplify the ratio by multiplying through by 3.
Total parts = 6+2+3 = 11. B's share = 2/11 × 6600.

solution.shortcut: Express all capitals in terms of one variable, find ratio, then apply to profit.
solution.commonMistake: Misreading 'B invests two-thirds of C' as 'C invests two-thirds of B'.

modelName: Simple Partnership – Ratio from Verbal Conditions

formulas.title: Simple Partnership Profit Ratio
formulas.expression: \text{A's profit} : \text{B's profit} : \text{C's profit} = A : B : C

questionText: A, B and C enter into a partnership with capitals in the ratio $\frac{7}{2} : \frac{4}{3} : \frac{6}{5}$. After 4 months A increases his share of capital by 50%. If at the end of the year the total profit earned is $\rupee 2430$, find the share of each in the profit.
correctAnswer: A's share = ₹1575, B's share = ₹450, C's share = ₹405

steps.explanation: Convert the ratio 7/2 : 4/3 : 6/5 to integers by multiplying by LCM of denominators (LCM of 2,3,5 = 30).
Let initial capitals be ₹105x, ₹40x, ₹36x. After 4 months, A increases capital by 50%, so A's new capital = 150% of 105x = 157.5x.
steps.calculation: A for first 4 months: 105x, for last 8 months: 157.5x

steps.explanation: Compute effective capital (capital × time) for each partner.
Simplify ratio by dividing by 48: 35 : 10 : 9. Total = 54.
Distribute profit ₹2430 in ratio 35:10:9.

solution.shortcut: Convert fractional capital ratio to integers using LCM, then apply compound partnership formula.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that A's capital increase applies only for the remaining months, not the full year.

modelName: Compound Partnership – Fractional Ratio and Mid-year Capital Increase

questionText: A, B and C enter into partnership with capitals of $\rupee 25000$, $\rupee 30000$ and $\rupee 15000$ respectively. A is the working partner and he gets 30% of the profit for managing the business. The balance profit is distributed in proportion to the capital investment. At the year-end, A gets $\rupee 200$ more than B and C together. Find the total profit and the share of each.
correctAnswer: Total profit = ₹2000; A's share = ₹1100, B's share = ₹600, C's share = ₹300

steps.explanation: Let total profit = ₹x. A gets 30% for managing = ₹(3x/10). Remaining balance = ₹(7x/10).
Ratio of capitals = 25000:30000:15000 = 5:6:3. Total parts = 14. A's share from balance = 5/14 × 7x/10; B's share = 6/14 × 7x/10; C's share = 3/14 × 7x/10.
A gets ₹200 more than B and C together. B+C = 3x/10 + 3x/20 = 9x/20. Equation: 11x/20 − 9x/20 = 200.

solution.shortcut: Separate the working partner's management fee before distributing the remaining profit by capital ratio.
solution.commonMistake: Applying profit ratio to the full profit including management fee instead of only the balance.

modelName: Partnership – Working Partner with Management Fee

questionText: Four milkmen rented a pasture. A grazed 24 cows for 3 months; B 10 cows for 5 months; C 35 cows for 4 months and D 21 cows for 3 months. If A's share of rent is $\rupee 720$, find the total rent of the field.

steps.explanation: Calculate effective usage (animals × months) for each milkman.
Total parts = 72+50+140+63 = 325. A's share = 72/325 of total rent.

solution.shortcut: Treat the problem as compound partnership: effective contribution = animals × time.
solution.commonMistake: Not including all four milkmen in the total parts when computing A's fraction.

questionText: Two persons A and B take a field on rent. A puts on it 21 horses for 3 months and 15 cows for 2 months; B puts 15 cows for 6 months and 40 sheep for $7\frac{1}{2}$ months. If, in one day, 3 horses eat as much as 5 cows eat and 6 cows as much as 10 sheep, what part of the rent should A pay?

steps.explanation: Establish equivalences. 6 cows = 10 sheep, so 1 cow = 5/3 sheep. 3 horses = 5 cows, so 1 horse = 5/3 cows = (5/3)(5/3) sheep = 25/9 sheep.
Convert A's animals to sheep-equivalent and compute effective usage.
Convert B's animals to sheep-equivalent and compute effective usage.
Ratio of A to B = 225 : 450 = 1 : 2. A's share of rent = 1/(1+2) = 1/3.

solution.shortcut: Convert all animals to a common unit (sheep) using given equivalences, then apply animal×month ratios.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to convert mixed fractions like 7½ months correctly, or misapplying the equivalence chain.

modelName: Partnership – Animal Equivalence and Rent Sharing

questionText: A, B and C took a house on rent for one year for $\rupee 13824$. They remained together for 4 months and then C left the house. After 5 more months, B also left the house. How much rent should each pay?

steps.explanation: Calculate monthly rent = 13824/12 = ₹1152.
First 4 months: all three together. Rent = 1152×4 = ₹4608. Each pays 4608/3 = ₹1536.
Next 5 months (months 5–9): A and B together. Rent = 1152×5 = ₹5760. Each pays 5760/2 = ₹2880.
Last 3 months (months 10–12): A alone. Rent = 1152×3 = ₹3456 paid by A.

solution.shortcut: Break the year into segments based on when people leave, divide rent equally among occupants in each segment.
solution.commonMistake: Applying profit-sharing ratios by capital rather than time-based equal sharing for housing rent.

modelName: Partnership – House Rent Split by Occupancy Period

questionText: A invested $\rupee 76000$ in a business. After few months, B joined him with $\rupee 57000$. At the end of the year, the total profit was divided between them in the ratio 2 : 1. After how many months did B join?

steps.explanation: Let B join after x months. Then B's money was invested for (12 – x) months. A invested for 12 months.
steps.calculation: A invested for 12 months, B for (12−x) months

steps.explanation: Set up the profit ratio equation: (76000×12) / (57000×(12−x)) = 2/1.
Solve for x: 12−x = 8 → x = 4.

solution.shortcut: Set up: (A's capital × 12) / (B's capital × (12−x)) = given profit ratio, then solve for x.
solution.commonMistake: Using x as the number of months B invested instead of when B joined.

modelName: Compound Partnership – Find Time of Joining

formulas.title: Profit Ratio from Compound Partnership
formulas.expression: \frac{A's\ capital \times T_A}{B's\ capital \times T_B} = \frac{\text{A's profit}}{\text{B's profit}}

questionText: The ratio of investments of two partners A and B is 11 : 12 and the ratio of their profits is 2 : 3. If A invested the money for 8 months, then for how much time B invested his money?

steps.explanation: Let A invest ₹11x for 8 months and B invest ₹12x for y months.
Use profit ratio: (11x×8)/(12x×y) = 2/3.
Cross-multiply: 88×3 = 24y → 264 = 24y → y = 11.

solution.shortcut: Profit ratio = (capital × time) ratio. Set up equation and solve for unknown time.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing capital ratio with effective capital (capital × time).

modelName: Compound Partnership – Find Duration of Investment

formulas.title: Profit Ratio from Investment and Time

questionText: A, B and C are partners in a business. A, whose money has been used for 4 months, claims $\frac{1}{8}$ of the profit. B, whose money has been used for 6 months, claims $\frac{1}{3}$ of the profit. C had invested $\rupee 1560$ for 8 months. How much money did A and B contribute?

steps.explanation: Let total profit = ₹x. A's share = x/8; B's share = x/3; C's share = x − x/8 − x/3.
Ratio of profits A:B:C = x/8 : x/3 : 13x/24 = 3 : 8 : 13 (multiply each by 24/x).
Since profit ratio = effective capital ratio, let A invest ₹a for 4 months and B invest ₹b for 6 months. Then: 4a : 6b : (1560×8) = 3 : 8 : 13.
Find B's capital similarly.

solution.shortcut: Convert profit fractions to a common denominator to get the profit ratio, then back-calculate capitals.
solution.commonMistake: Using the profit shares as capital ratios directly without dividing by investment time.

modelName: Partnership – Find Capital from Profit Fraction and Time

formulas.title: Effective Capital from Profit Ratio
formulas.expression: \text{Capital} = \frac{\text{Profit ratio part}}{\text{Reference part}} \times \frac{\text{Reference capital} \times \text{Reference time}}{\text{Own time}}

questionText: A, B and C enter into a partnership by investing in the ratio of 3 : 2 : 4. After one year, B invests another $\rupee 270000$ and C, at the end of 2 years, also invests $\rupee 270000$. At the end of three years, profits are shared in the ratio of 3 : 4 : 5. Find the initial investment of each.
correctAnswer: A's initial investment = ₹270000, B's initial investment = ₹180000, C's initial investment = ₹360000

steps.explanation: Let initial investments be ₹3x, ₹2x and ₹4x for A, B and C respectively. Calculate effective capital over 3 years for each.
steps.calculation: A invests 3x for all 36 months; B invests 2x for first 12 months then (2x+270000) for next 24 months; C invests 4x for first 24 months then (4x+270000) for last 12 months.

steps.explanation: Write effective capital expressions.
Set ratio equal to 3:4:5. Use A and B: 108x/(72x+6480000) = 3/4.

solution.shortcut: Form the effective capital equation for each partner over the full period and match the given profit ratio.
solution.commonMistake: Adding the additional investment for the wrong time period or forgetting to carry it forward to the end.

modelName: Compound Partnership – Additional Investment and Find Initial Capital

questionText: A, B and C enter into a partnership. Their capital contribution is in the ratio 21 : 18 : 14. At the end of the business term they share profits in the ratio 15 : 8 : 9. Find the ratio of time for which they invest their capitals.

steps.explanation: Let A invest ₹21x for p months, B invest ₹18x for q months and C invest ₹14x for r months.
Express ratio p:q:r with q as reference. Multiply through by 56/q: p:q:r = 45×56/28 : 56 : 81 = 90 : 56 : 81.

solution.shortcut: Use profit = capital × time, express each time in terms of one common variable and simplify the ratio.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing capital ratio with profit ratio when setting up equations.

modelName: Partnership – Find Time Ratio from Capital and Profit Ratios

formulas.title: Time Ratio from Capital and Profit Ratios
